title: Make-Good Obligations: What You Owe at the End of Your Lease
metaTitle: Make-Good Obligations on a Commercial Lease: Costs and How to Limit Them | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Make-good can cost $50–$200/sqm at lease end. Here's what landlords typically require, what it costs, and how to limit your obligation at the start.
excerpt: Make-good obligations catch many tenants off guard at the end of a commercial lease. Here's what's typically required, what it costs, and how to negotiate the clause before you sign.
author: Joe Kelley
body:
## What is a make-good obligation?

A make-good obligation is a clause in your commercial lease requiring you to return the premises to a specified condition when the lease ends. In its broadest form, it can require you to strip out your entire fitout and return the space to its original base-building condition. In a more limited form, it might only require you to repaint, replace carpet, and remove signage.

The difference between a broad and a limited make-good clause can be tens of thousands of dollars.

## What do landlords typically require?

Make-good obligations vary by landlord, building, and negotiation. The most common requirements fall into two categories:

### Full reinstatement
The tenant must return the space exactly as it was at the start of the lease — stripped back to the base building. This includes removing partition walls, ceilings, data cabling, fitout joinery, and any other works the tenant installed. This is expensive.

### Cosmetic make-good
The tenant is required to repaint walls, replace worn carpet, remove furniture and signage, and leave the space in a clean, presentable state. They are not required to remove fitout improvements.

Many leases sit somewhere between these two — for example, requiring removal of partitions but not the ceiling grid, or requiring the tenant to pay a cash settlement in lieu of works.

## What does make-good cost?

Costs depend heavily on what you're required to do and the extent of your fitout.

| Make-Good Scope | Approximate Cost per sqm |
|---|---|
| Cosmetic only (paint, carpet, clean) | $30–$60/sqm |
| Partial strip (remove partitions, patch, repaint) | $60–$100/sqm |
| Full reinstatement to base building | $100–$200/sqm |
| Complex fitout (servers, raised floors, specialist works) | $150–$300/sqm |

On a 500 sqm office with a full reinstatement obligation, you could be looking at $50,000–$100,000 at the end of the lease. That's a liability you need to plan for — and ideally negotiate down before you sign.

## Common make-good disputes

Disputes arise when:

- The lease uses vague language like "original condition" without defining what that means
- No condition report was done at the start of the lease, so there's no agreed baseline
- The landlord attempts to recover costs beyond what's covered by the clause
- The tenant leaves without doing the required works and the landlord goes ahead and does them, then invoices the tenant

These disputes are time-consuming and expensive. The best way to avoid them is to be precise at the start.

## How to negotiate make-good before you sign

### 1. Define the obligation precisely

Don't accept vague language. The lease should clearly state exactly what the tenant is and isn't required to do. "Return to original condition" is not sufficient. Get it defined: paint, carpet, partition removal, signage removal — itemise it.

### 2. Commission a condition report at commencement

Get an independent condition report done at the start of the lease. Both parties sign off on it. This establishes the agreed baseline and removes ambiguity about what "original condition" actually means.

### 3. Exclude specific approved works

If you're doing a fitout and the landlord is contributing to it, negotiate that the works they've approved don't need to be removed at lease end — or that the make-good only applies to works done without their approval.

### 4. Cap the make-good cost

Some leases allow for a cash settlement in lieu of physical works. Negotiate a cap on the make-good liability (e.g. no more than $X per sqm, regardless of actual cost). This gives you cost certainty.

### 5. Include a cash-out option

Negotiate the right to pay the landlord an agreed cash amount instead of performing the works. This is often cheaper than having the landlord's contractor do it.

## Get a make-good assessment early

If your lease is expiring in the next 6–12 months, don't wait until the last month to assess your make-good obligation. Get an independent assessment now so you know what you're facing, whether you can negotiate a settlement, and how to plan your exit.

If you're about to sign a lease, talk to us before you do. The make-good clause is one of the most negotiable parts of a commercial lease — but only before you sign it.
